skill.name: html-injection-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized HTML injection testing, including discovery, proof of concept validation, impact analysis, and remediation guidance.
skill.summary: Assess HTML injection exposure and document remediation for authorized web security testing.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly markdown examples, but semantic review confirms dangerous instructional content. The skill explicitly teaches phishing form construction, credential capture endpoints, defacement payloads, redirect and iframe injection, filter bypasses, and automated probing. No prompt injection attempt was found, but the offensive workflow supports misuse and should be blocked from publication.

critical_findings.title: Credential Phishing and Capture Instructions
critical_findings.description: The skill provides fake login form overlays and forms that submit usernames and passwords to attacker-controlled endpoints. This goes beyond vulnerability validation and directly enables credential theft.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is explicitly titled phishing attack construction and contains credential fields submitted to attacker URLs. The stated purpose includes stealing user credentials through injected forms.

critical_findings.title: Defacement Payload Guidance
critical_findings.description: The skill teaches full-page overlays, body replacement, and injected imagery intended to alter a target website appearance. These instructions can support unauthorized defacement.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is explicitly about defacement payloads and includes visible full-page replacement examples. The front matter also names defacing web applications as an intended use.

high_findings.title: Bypass and XSS Escalation Techniques
high_findings.description: The skill includes case changes, encoding variants, tag splitting, null bytes, event handlers, and script tag examples. These techniques help evade filters and can escalate HTML injection into script execution testing.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section is specifically framed as filter evasion and includes executable event-handler payloads. Legitimate testing is possible, but the content materially improves offensive capability.

high_findings.title: Network Exfiltration and Redirect Payloads
high_findings.description: The skill demonstrates attacker-controlled links, images, CSS callbacks, meta redirects, form hijacking, and iframe loading. These patterns can send users or data to external attacker infrastructure.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple examples use attacker or evil domains for links, callbacks, redirects, form actions, and iframe sources. The intent is clear enough to treat the network findings as confirmed risk.

medium_findings.title: Automated Active Testing Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill provides curl commands, Burp Suite and ZAP workflows, and a Python requests-based fuzzer. These are legitimate for authorized testing, but they enable active probing against web applications.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and script are clearly examples rather than hidden code execution. Risk remains because they automate probing and include malicious payloads.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe DOM Sink Example
medium_findings.description: The remediation section correctly labels direct innerHTML assignment as dangerous and then shows sanitized use with DOMPurify. The unsafe sink is educational but still a risky copyable pattern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static innerHTML finding is contextually educational, and the file calls it dangerous. It is not hidden malicious code, but it remains a copyable unsafe pattern.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown
low_findings.description: Many external command and weak cryptography alerts appear to come from markdown fences, payload text, or unrelated words rather than executable skill code. These do not reduce the confirmed semantic risk.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content is markdown front matter or fenced example text, not executable runtime code. The false positives are distinguishable from the confirmed phishing and defacement guidance.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Keyword False Positive
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM database alerts do not correspond to credential database access in the reviewed lines. The nearby content is a defacement heading and general severity discussion.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: I did not find evidence of Windows SAM database access at the reported locations. The alert appears to be a keyword or tokenization match, not a real sensitive file access pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Fake Login Form Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: Credential collection forms are shown as payloads for injected HTML. This is a direct phishing pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The payload contains username and password inputs and attacker-controlled form actions. The surrounding text describes phishing attack construction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filter Evasion Payloads
dangerous_patterns.description: Encoding, tag splitting, null-byte, and event-handler variants are provided to evade basic defenses.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section explicitly says to evade filters and lists bypass techniques. The examples are directly usable in injection testing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Attacker-Controlled External Endpoints
dangerous_patterns.description: Payloads repeatedly point links, images, forms, CSS, redirects, and iframes to attacker-controlled hosts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external endpoints appear across multiple payload classes and are tied to capture, steal, tracking, and redirect behavior. This confirms the network risk semantically.

content.value_statement: HTML injection can allow page manipulation, phishing, and misleading content. This skill organizes authorized testing steps and remediation notes, but its current source includes unsafe attack guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains reflected, stored, POST, URL-based, CSS, meta, form, and iframe injection contexts.
Lists common input surfaces and parameters that may reflect unsafe HTML.
Shows manual validation workflows using a browser, proxy, curl, Burp Suite, and OWASP ZAP.
Describes impact categories such as phishing, defacement, redirects, and content manipulation.
Includes remediation guidance for output encoding, allowlists, CSP, WAF rules, and DOM sanitization.
limitations: The source includes explicit phishing and credential capture payloads, so it is not safe for unrestricted publication.
It does not enforce authorization checks or scope controls before testing targets.
It does not provide a complete legal or reporting workflow for professional assessments.
Examples may become outdated as browsers, frameworks, and WAF behavior change.

use_cases.title: Validate a Client Web Form
use_cases.description: Confirm whether user-controlled fields render HTML and document the business impact with remediation advice.

use_cases.title: Review Output Encoding Controls
use_cases.description: Compare application behavior against expected escaping, sanitization, and CSP protections.

use_cases.title: Prepare Developer Remediation Notes
use_cases.description: Translate an HTML injection finding into clear fixes for frontend and backend teams.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Authorized Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a safe HTML injection test plan for this authorized application. Include scope, inputs to check, and non-destructive payload categories.

prompt_templates.title: Triage Reflected HTML
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this reflected HTML behavior and explain whether it is HTML injection, XSS, or safe encoding. Include remediation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Finding
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a professional vulnerability finding for confirmed HTML injection. Include impact, evidence summary, severity, and recommended fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Assess Defense Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these HTML injection defenses and identify gaps across output encoding, sanitization, CSP, WAF rules, and regression tests.

output_examples.input: A search parameter reflects formatted HTML into the response.
output_examples.output: The result should classify the issue, explain user impact, note whether script execution is possible, and recommend context-aware output encoding.

output_examples.input: A profile field stores user-controlled markup that appears on public pages.
output_examples.output: The result should describe stored HTML injection risk, affected users, proof summary, severity factors, and safe remediation steps.

output_examples.input: A team wants to prevent repeat HTML injection findings.
output_examples.output: The result should propose regression tests, safer rendering APIs, sanitizer configuration review, and security acceptance criteria.

best_practices: Use only on systems where testing is explicitly authorized and scoped.
Prefer harmless visual markers for proof of concept validation.
Pair every confirmed issue with context-aware encoding or sanitization guidance.
anti_patterns: Do not create phishing forms, credential capture endpoints, or deceptive overlays.
Do not test public or third-party targets without written permission.
Do not copy unsafe payloads into production systems as demonstrations.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish as submitted?
faq.answer: No. The current source includes phishing, credential capture, defacement, and bypass instructions.

faq.question: Can HTML injection be legitimate to test?
faq.answer: Yes, when testing is authorized, scoped, non-destructive, and focused on remediation.

faq.question: How is HTML injection different from XSS?
faq.answer: HTML injection renders markup. XSS executes script. HTML injection can still support phishing and defacement.

faq.question: What evidence should a report include?
faq.answer: Include the affected input, response behavior, screenshot or transcript summary, impact, severity rationale, and remediation steps.

faq.question: What fixes are usually recommended?
faq.answer: Use context-aware output encoding, safe rendering APIs, allowlisted sanitization, CSP, and regression tests.

faq.question: What should be removed before publication?
faq.answer: Remove credential theft payloads, deceptive phishing forms, defacement examples, and bypass instructions that enable misuse.
